findings.title: AWS-format access key ID hardcoded in test fixture
findings.evidence: New file adds `export const fakeAwsKey = "AKIA0123456789ABCDEF";` — a literal matching the AWS access key ID format (AKIA prefix + 16 alphanumerics). Diff Drift reported '0 active flags' and listed the file as '1 added · 0 active flags', so it suppressed/missed this secret-shaped literal. Although named 'fake' and located under tests/fixtures, the value is exported and well-formed; it warrants human confirmation that no real key was committed.

notes: Diff Drift analyzed the file (tests/fixtures/provider.test.ts — '1 added · 0 active flags') but raised no risk flag, indicating its heuristics suppress secret-shaped literals in test/fixture paths. This is the central behavior under review.

The literal AKIA0123456789ABCDEF is structurally valid for an AWS access key ID but uses an obviously sequential/placeholder body and is named `fakeAwsKey`, which supports it being a benign fixture rather than a live credential.
The diff is a single-line new file with no executable logic, network calls, or dependency changes; risk is confined to the presence of a credential-shaped string.

Decision is 'investigate' rather than 'block' because the strong evidence of it being a deliberate dummy (name + sequential pattern) is offset by Diff Drift silently not flagging an AKIA-format string, which a reviewer should confirm.

evaluator.note: Benchmark v4 blind run against the engine-v2 binary; packet-only context.
